panel.origin: The opening in a lens that controls how much light enters, measured in f-stops. It also governs depth of field — how much of the image is in focus.
panel.mixology: She opened the aperture to f/1.8 to blur the background and let in more light.
panel.examples: From Latin 'apertura' (an opening). Confusingly, smaller f-numbers mean a wider opening — f/1.8 is wide open, f/16 is a tiny pinhole.

panel.origin: How long the camera's shutter stays open to expose the sensor, measured in fractions of a second. It controls motion — freezing or blurring it.
panel.mixology: A fast shutter speed of 1/2000 froze the splashing water in midair.
panel.examples: Descriptive of how quickly the shutter opens and closes. Fast speeds freeze action; slow speeds blur it, capturing motion as streaks of light.

panel.origin: The sensor's sensitivity to light. Low ISO gives clean images in bright light; high ISO brightens dark scenes but adds graininess ('noise').
panel.mixology: He cranked the ISO to 6400 to shoot the dim concert, accepting some grain for a usable shot.
panel.examples: Named for the International Organization for Standardization, which standardized film sensitivity ratings — a system carried over to digital sensors.

panel.origin: The overall brightness of an image, determined by the balance of aperture, shutter speed, and ISO — the 'exposure triangle.' Too much is overexposed; too little, under.
panel.mixology: She nailed the exposure, capturing detail in both the bright sky and the shadowed rocks.
panel.examples: From 'expose' — the sensor or film is 'exposed' to light. The three controls trade off against each other, the fundamental balancing act of photography.

panel.origin: A unit of light measurement. Each 'stop' doubles or halves the light — a key concept tying together aperture, shutter, and ISO adjustments.
panel.mixology: He brightened the shot by one stop, doubling the light hitting the sensor.
panel.examples: From the historical lens 'stops' (physical plates with holes). One stop is a doubling or halving of light — the universal currency of exposure adjustments.

panel.origin: 'Overexposed' images are too bright, losing detail in the highlights; 'underexposed' images are too dark, losing detail in the shadows. Both lose information.
panel.mixology: The snowy scene came out overexposed, the bright drifts washed to featureless white.
panel.examples: Descriptive: 'over' or 'under' the ideal amount of exposure. Lost highlight or shadow detail often can't be recovered, making correct exposure critical.

panel.origin: How the camera measures the light in a scene to suggest an exposure. Different metering modes weigh the frame differently — the whole scene, the center, or a spot.
panel.mixology: She switched to spot metering to expose correctly for the backlit subject's face.
panel.examples: From 'meter' (to measure). The light meter reads the scene's brightness; the mode determines which parts of the frame it prioritizes.

panel.origin: A graph showing the distribution of brightness in an image, from dark (left) to light (right). A precise tool for judging exposure beyond the screen.
panel.mixology: The histogram bunched against the right edge, warning him the highlights were clipping.
panel.examples: From Greek 'histos' (mast, web) + 'gramma' (drawing) — a statistical bar chart adapted to show how an image's tones are distributed.

panel.origin: The lens measurement (in millimeters) that determines magnification and angle of view. Short focal lengths see wide; long ones zoom in tight.
panel.mixology: He switched to a 200mm focal length to fill the frame with the distant bird.
panel.examples: Technically the distance from the lens's optical center to the sensor when focused at infinity. In practice, it's the number that tells you how 'zoomed in' a lens is.

panel.origin: How much of the image, from front to back, appears in sharp focus. 'Shallow' depth blurs all but the subject; 'deep' depth keeps everything sharp.
panel.mixology: A shallow depth of field threw the busy background into a creamy blur, isolating the portrait.
panel.examples: Descriptive: the 'depth' of the zone that's in 'field' (focus). Controlled mainly by aperture, it's a primary creative tool for directing the viewer's eye.

panel.origin: The aesthetic quality of the out-of-focus areas in an image, especially blurred points of light rendered as soft orbs. Prized for its dreamy look.
panel.mixology: The fairy lights melted into beautiful round bokeh behind the subject.
panel.examples: From Japanese 'boke' (blur, haze). It refers not to the amount of blur but its pleasing quality — the character of how a lens renders what's out of focus.

panel.origin: A 'prime' lens has one fixed focal length; a 'zoom' lens covers a range. Primes are sharper and faster; zooms are more versatile.
panel.mixology: She loved her prime for its razor sharpness but packed a zoom for travel flexibility.
panel.examples: 'Prime' from the sense of fixed or fundamental; 'zoom' from the variable lens that lets you zoom in and out. A classic tradeoff of quality versus convenience.

panel.origin: A 'wide-angle' lens captures a broad view, exaggerating space; a 'telephoto' lens magnifies distant subjects, compressing space. Opposite ends of the lens spectrum.
panel.mixology: He shot the sweeping landscape wide, then switched to telephoto to isolate a distant peak.
panel.examples: 'Wide' for the broad angle of view; 'telephoto' from Greek 'tele' (far) + 'photo' (light) — a lens that photographs faraway things up close.

panel.origin: Extreme close-up photography that captures tiny subjects at life-size or greater magnification, revealing detail invisible to the naked eye.
panel.mixology: Her macro shot of the dewdrop revealed a tiny world reflected inside it.
panel.examples: From Greek 'makros' (large) — paradoxically, it makes small things appear large, magnifying insects, flowers, and textures to dramatic scale.

panel.origin: The adjustment that makes the subject appear sharp and clear. A photo can be in focus, out of focus, or selectively focused on one element.
panel.mixology: He locked focus on the runner's eyes, letting the rest of the frame fall soft.
panel.examples: From Latin 'focus' (hearth, fireplace) — later adopted in optics for the point where light rays converge to form a sharp image.

panel.origin: The setting that keeps colors accurate under different light sources, so white looks white whether under sunlight, shade, or indoor bulbs.
panel.mixology: She corrected the white balance to remove the orange cast from the tungsten lamps.
panel.examples: Descriptive: it 'balances' the image so whites render neutrally. Without it, photos pick up color casts — bluish in shade, orange under household bulbs.

panel.origin: The period shortly after sunrise and before sunset when light is soft, warm, and golden. Photographers' favorite natural light.
panel.mixology: They scheduled the shoot for golden hour, when the low sun bathed everything in warm light.
panel.examples: Named for the warm, golden quality of low-angle sunlight. The sun's low position softens shadows and casts a flattering glow prized for portraits and landscapes.

panel.origin: 'Hard light' is direct and creates sharp, dark shadows and high contrast; 'soft light' is diffused, gentle, and creates gradual shadows. Two fundamental light qualities.
panel.mixology: He diffused the harsh flash into soft light to flatter the subject's skin.
panel.examples: Descriptive of how light renders shadows. A small or distant source (like the bare sun) is 'hard'; a large or diffused one (like an overcast sky) is 'soft'.

panel.origin: Light coming from behind the subject, toward the camera. It can create silhouettes, glowing rim-light, or dramatic haze.
panel.mixology: The backlight rimmed her hair with a glowing halo against the dark background.
panel.examples: Descriptive: the light is 'behind' the subject. Tricky to expose for, but it produces some of photography's most striking, luminous effects.

panel.origin: A secondary light (or reflector) used to brighten shadows created by the main light, reducing harsh contrast. It 'fills in' the dark areas.
panel.mixology: A reflector bounced fill light into the shadows, softening the contrast on his face.
panel.examples: Descriptive: it 'fills' the shadows with light. A core tool of lighting setups, balancing the brightness between the lit and shadowed sides of a subject.

panel.origin: Color Temperature — a measure (in Kelvin) of how warm (orange) or cool (blue) a light source is. Candlelight is warm; midday sky is cool.
panel.mixology: The warm 3000K bulbs gave the room a cozy glow, far warmer than cool daylight.
panel.examples: Borrowed from physics, where heating an object shifts its glow from red to blue. Counterintuitively, lower Kelvin numbers look warmer, higher numbers cooler.

panel.origin: The range of brightness a camera can capture, from the darkest shadow to the brightest highlight, with detail retained. Wider range handles high-contrast scenes better.
panel.mixology: The camera's wide dynamic range held detail in both the bright window and the dim room.
panel.examples: From 'dynamic' (range of variation) — the span between the faintest and strongest signal the sensor can record before losing detail to pure black or white.

panel.origin: A guideline that divides the frame into a 3×3 grid; placing key elements along the lines or intersections creates more balanced, dynamic images.
panel.mixology: She placed the horizon on the lower third line rather than dead center for a more pleasing balance.
panel.examples: A simplification of older artistic ideas about balanced composition. The off-center placement tends to feel more natural and engaging than centering everything.

panel.origin: Lines within an image (roads, fences, rivers) that guide the viewer's eye toward the subject or through the scene. A powerful compositional tool.
panel.mixology: The winding road served as a leading line, drawing the eye toward the mountains beyond.
panel.examples: Descriptive: the lines 'lead' the viewer's gaze. Photographers hunt for natural lines to create depth and direct attention exactly where they want it.

panel.origin: Using elements in the scene (a doorway, branches, an arch) to surround and draw attention to the subject. A frame within the frame.
panel.mixology: He used the stone archway for framing, the subject perfectly enclosed within it.
panel.examples: From the literal 'frame' — a foreground element that encloses the subject, adding depth, context, and focus, like a built-in picture frame.

panel.origin: The empty or uncluttered area around a subject. Generous negative space can make a subject stand out and give an image room to breathe.
panel.mixology: Vast negative space of empty sky left the lone tree small but powerfully isolated.
panel.examples: From art theory, where 'negative space' is the area around and between subjects. Used deliberately, emptiness becomes a powerful compositional element.

panel.origin: A balanced composition where elements mirror each other across an axis. Symmetry creates a sense of order, harmony, and sometimes striking formality.
panel.mixology: The reflection in the still lake created perfect symmetry, top and bottom mirroring exactly.
panel.examples: From Greek 'symmetria' (agreement in measure). Our eyes are drawn to symmetry; photographers use it for impact, or break it deliberately for tension.

panel.origin: 'Foreground' is what's nearest the camera; 'background' is what's farthest. Layering them creates depth in a two-dimensional image.
panel.mixology: He placed flowers in the foreground and mountains in the background to build a sense of depth.
panel.examples: Descriptive: the 'fore' (front) and 'back' grounds of the scene. Using both gives a flat photograph the illusion of three-dimensional depth.

panel.origin: Vantage Point — the position and angle from which a photo is taken. Shooting from high, low, or unexpected angles transforms an ordinary subject.
panel.mixology: By crouching for a low vantage point, she made the small dog look heroic against the sky.
panel.examples: From Old French 'avantage' (advantage) — a 'vantage' point offers an advantageous view. Changing it is the simplest way to make a tired subject fresh.

panel.origin: 'RAW' files capture all the sensor's data for maximum editing flexibility; 'JPEG' files are compressed and processed, smaller but less editable. A core workflow choice.
panel.mixology: She shot in RAW to have full latitude to recover highlights and adjust color later.
panel.examples: 'RAW' (not an acronym, just the word) signals the unprocessed sensor data; JPEG is named for the Joint Photographic Experts Group that created the format.

panel.origin: Obsessively examining an image at extreme magnification to scrutinize fine detail and sharpness. Often a sign of fussing over gear instead of making pictures.
panel.mixology: He spent the evening pixel-peeping, zooming to 200% to compare lens sharpness corner to corner.
panel.examples: A faintly mocking term for inspecting individual pixels — gear enthusiasts obsessing over technical minutiae that viewers would never notice.

panel.origin: Repeatedly checking the camera's rear screen after each shot, often with delight. The habit of reviewing instead of staying ready for the next moment.
panel.mixology: Stop chimping and keep shooting — you'll miss the action staring at your last frame.
panel.examples: Said to come from the 'ooh-ooh' noises photographers make admiring their shots, likened to chimpanzees — a gently teasing bit of photographer slang.

panel.origin: Affectionate nickname for a 50mm prime lens, beloved for being cheap, sharp, fast, and versatile. Often a photographer's first 'real' lens upgrade.
panel.mixology: Every beginner should grab a nifty fifty — it's the best value lens you can buy.
panel.examples: Rhyming slang celebrating the 50mm lens's 'nifty' combination of low price and high quality, a near-universal recommendation for new photographers.

panel.origin: Describes highlights so overexposed they've lost all detail, turning pure featureless white. Unrecoverable information, a common exposure error.
panel.mixology: The bright sky was completely blown out, just a sheet of white where clouds should be.
panel.examples: From the sense of detail being 'blown out' or obliterated by excess light — the brightest areas clip to pure white with nothing left to recover.

panel.origin: Describes an image in perfect, crisp focus — as sharp as a tack. The highest praise for image sharpness.
panel.mixology: Shot on a tripod at the right settings, the landscape came out tack-sharp from edge to edge.
panel.examples: From the sharpness of a tack's point — vivid slang for an image so crisply focused that fine details are rendered with perfect clarity.
